message.content:
Adversaries may leverage AppInit DLLs for persistence and privilege escalation by loading malicious DLLs into processes via the AppInit_DLLs registry value. This technique ensures that the malicious DLL is loaded into nearly every process, as it targets the ubiquitous file library. This method is akin to Process Injection, allowing adversaries to execute code with elevated privileges within the context of other processes. AppInit DLLs can also provide continuous persistence by being triggered through API activity. Notably, this functionality is disabled in Windows 8 and later versions when secure boot is enabled. 

Adversaries such as APT39, Cherry Picker, Ramsay, and T9000 have exploited this technique to establish persistence by modifying specific registry keys. Mitigation strategies include enabling secure boot on Windows 8 and later systems, monitoring and auditing registry changes, and employing endpoint detection and response (EDR) solutions to detect and block suspicious DLL loading activities. Regularly updating and patching systems can also help prevent exploitation of this technique.
